narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 39, which stands out above the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 57.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, which is below the average of 3.21 and had the strongest pull toward forecasting failure, suggesting it signals potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4, also under the typical 5.53 average, reinforced this concern as the next biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. Your middle family income bracket played a moderate role in the same direction, possibly indicating fewer resources compared to higher-income peers who often succeed. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 4 had a smaller but similar negative influence, while your full-time enrollment status nudged things slightly toward success, aligning with the vast majority of students. Your LSAT score was the standout positive force, countering the risks more than any other factor and highlighting your aptitude for the logical demands of law. Compared to typical students, your academic deciles and GPA are in the lower half, which weighed heavily against you despite the LSAT strength. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted growth. To move forward, focus on bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or explaining context in reapplications, retake the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and seek programs with strong bar prep support for underrepresented students like yourself. You're capable of turning this around; consider reaching out to mentors who've succeeded against odds. Keep pushing—law needs your voice.
